text: This video describes how robotic surgery can be very helpful in managing complex biliary duct lesions. The patient is a seventy-six year old female with a biliary structure nine years after laparoscopic polycystectomy. The structure was classified as a bismuth-Strasberg E-tree lesion.

Four robotic 8mm ports and one assistant 12mm port are positioned for the procedure. During hilar dissection, the right and left hepatic ducts in the stenotic area are clearly visible. The surgeon removes old hemostatic clips from the ductus and polymer clips near the common hepatic duct and hilum.

The surgeon transects the left hepatic duct and resects the common duct below the stenosis using a GIAS tapering device. The right and left hepatic ducts are sutured together in a side-by-side manner to form a double parallel connection.

The surgeon constructs the small intestine 30 centimeters distal from the Treitz ligament, pulling the distal limb upwards to perform a hepatico-jejunostomy. Using the double barrel technique, an end-to-side hepatico-jejunostomy anastomosis is created. The posterior layers are sutured with a running stitch using resorbable V-lock sutures, followed by suturing the anterior layers with a second V-lock. Gradual tension is applied using two separate sutures: a resorbable 2-0 barbed wire for the outer layer and a 3-0 barbed wire for the inner layer.
A silicon drain is placed in the surgical field. The distal limb of the UNA reconstruction is marked with Chinese ink.
